region_of_origin: Global (almonds originate from the Middle East and Central Asia; widely consumed globally)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general almond entry; no specific baked/dry-roasted unsalted variant code exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00JL covers almonds as a commodity; no sub-code for baked/dry-roasted preparation. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT substance code for almond used; no preparation-specific code for baked/unsalted variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of baked unsalted almonds is approximately 30 g (about 23 whole almonds), providing roughly 179 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Baked almond, unsalted — HPB Singapore Foodo / Nutrient Composition of Foods in Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030002 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 170567, Almonds, dry roasted, without salt added) where HPB does not report those fields.

gi_source.title: Almonds, raw and roasted — Glycemic Index values from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 15 for almonds (roasted). Baked unsalted almonds are nutritionally equivalent to dry-roasted almonds with no added salt; this value is applied as a close analogous estimate. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (21.01 g/100g × 30 g serving = 6.3 g) / 100 = 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g) with high potassium (705 mg/100g), calcium (268 mg/100g), and magnesium (270 mg/100g) — all DASH-diet minerals that support blood pressure reduction.
hypertension.guidance: Baked unsalted almonds are well-suited for hypertensive individuals; a 30 g daily serving contributes meaningful DASH-diet minerals while delivering only 0.9 mg sodium, but patients must select unsalted varieties exclusively as salted almonds can contain 200–400 mg sodium per 100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), high dietary fibre (10.9 g/100g), and high protein (20.96 g/100g) collectively blunt postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 30 g portion of baked unsalted almonds produces a glycemic load of approximately 0.9, making them a clinically appropriate snack for type 2 diabetes management; however, portion control is essential given the high caloric density of 598 kcal/100g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable fatty acid profile: saturated fat is low (~4 g/100g) while monounsaturated fat (33.6 g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (12.3 g/100g) dominate, alongside high vitamin E (25.6 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (10.9 g/100g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of unsalted almonds (30 g/day) is supported by evidence for LDL-cholesterol reduction and improved endothelial function; the high MUFA/PUFA ratio and vitamin E content make them a heart-healthy snack when substituted for refined carbohydrate or saturated-fat-rich foods.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat well below 12 g/100g threshold; dominant MUFA/PUFA, high vitamin E (anti-inflammatory antioxidant), magnesium, and polyphenols support immune regulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Baked unsalted almonds qualify under the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out for rheumatoid arthritis; patients should avoid flavoured or honey-roasted variants which may contain added sugar and pro-inflammatory additives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked unsalted almonds (cr_id F09030002) are whole almonds (Prunus dulcis) that have been dry-baked or dry-roasted without the addition of salt or oil. The baking process deepens the natural nutty flavour, produces a firm and crunchy texture, and slightly intensifies the golden-brown colour of the skin. No salt, oil, or flavouring agents are added, making this a minimally processed whole-food snack. Per 100 g, baked unsalted almonds deliver 598 kcal, 20.96 g protein, 52.54 g total fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat at ~33.6 g and polyunsaturated fat at ~12.3 g, with only ~4 g saturated fat), 21.01 g carbohydrate, 4.86 g sugar, and 10.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100g. Notable micronutrients include calcium (268 mg), iron (3.73 mg), magnesium (270 mg), potassium (705 mg), phosphorus (484 mg), vitamin E (25.63 mg), and riboflavin (1.01 mg). The glycemic index is approximately 15 (low), yielding a glycemic load of only 0.9 per standard 30 g serving. Clinically, baked unsalted almonds are suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes (low GI, high fibre, high protein), hypertension (DASH-diet mineral profile, negligible sodium), and cardiovascular disease (favourable MUFA/PUFA ratio, LDL-lowering evidence). They are vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher. The primary allergen is tree nuts. A standard serving is approximately 30 g (~23 whole almonds), providing ~179 kcal.
common_user_queries: Are baked almonds good for diabetics?
How many calories in a handful of almonds?
Are unsalted almonds good for blood pressure?
What is the GI of almonds?
How much protein is in almonds?
What is a healthy almond serving size?
Are dry roasted almonds the same as baked almonds?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030002, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, fatty acid breakdown, and additional micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170567 (Almonds, dry roasted, without salt added), an approved secondary source. GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for roasted almonds, which is nutritionally equivalent to baked unsalted almonds.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.97 g), monounsaturated fat (33.61 g), polyunsaturated fat (12.33 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (705 mg), cholesterol (0.0 mg), vitamin C (0.0 mg), vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), thiamine (0.21 mg), riboflavin (1.01 mg), niacin (3.62 mg), vitamin B6 (0.14 mg), vitamin B12 (0.0 mcg), folate (44 mcg DFE), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), vitamin E (25.63 mg), phosphorus (484 mg), magnesium (270 mg), zinc (3.08 mg), selenium (4.1 mcg), and water (2.89 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170567 (Almonds, dry roasted, without salt added) as HPB does not report these fields. 2. No per_100g fields are set to -1; all fields have been populated from approved sources. 3. Core macros (protein_g: 20.96, fat_g: 52.54, carbohydrate_g: 21.01) are all non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification is required. 4. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for roasted almonds; baked unsalted almonds are nutritionally equivalent. GL per 30 g serving = 15 × 6.3 / 100 = 0.9. 5. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are close matches; no exact baked/dry-roasted unsalted almond code exists in either ontology as of 2026. 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6/Q8 multilingual rules. Aliases are English-only as appropriate for a globally generic nut product.
